text: The square celebrates a failed uprising with speeches by officials whose grandparents ordered the arrests. The old barricade stones are now decorative seating.

choices.text: Return the square to the survivors

outcomes.text: The ceremony becomes a meeting. The dead are remembered as people with unfinished demands, not safe symbols.
The medal honors resistance in a form guaranteed not to resist anything.
By night the benches have become a barricade again. History appreciates modular furniture.

text: The revolutionary committee has split into six factions over whether the meeting table is a tool of horizontal organization or a rectangle of hierarchy.

choices.text: Ask what each faction can do tomorrow

outcomes.text: Three groups build a kitchen, two print leaflets, and one continues fighting the table on theoretical grounds.
Everyone signs after adding contradictory footnotes. Unity survives as a well-organized disagreement.
Your business is the only institution to achieve cross-faction support.

text: A courier carries news that the capital's general strike has begun. Every checkpoint has been ordered to stop information because workers may misuse facts collectively.

outcomes.text: The news arrives as ten rumors that agree on the important verb: stop.

choices.text: Memorize the message and burn it

outcomes.text: One copy survives in your head, safe until fear or a club edits it.

choices.text: Sell the route to the checkpoint

outcomes.text: The courier is caught. The strike continues, poorer by one voice and richer in reasons.

text: A forest commune survived the monarchy and now risks losing every meeting to the founder who says shared power works best when he explains it alone.

outcomes.text: The founder loses office and keeps a home, proving accountability need not require a guillotine for every disagreement.
Meetings become shorter because only one person speaks. Stability learns to resemble ownership.

choices.text: Leave and start a purer commune

outcomes.text: You depart with twelve people and all the original problems, now freshly named.

text: The royal mine was nationalized after a revolt. The same overseer remains, now wearing a public badge and explaining that exploitation belongs to everyone.

choices.text: Elect the mine council from the shifts

outcomes.text: Public ownership gains worker power instead of a new letterhead.

choices.text: Report the overseer to the ministry

outcomes.text: The ministry replaces him with an inspector trained by the same manual.
Your badge belongs to the people. Your office door locks from the inside.

text: The tyrant is gone. The throne room holds weapons, tax records, keys, and a crowd asking the dangerous question that follows victory: who decides now?

outcomes.text: Decisions become slower, arguments multiply, and no single room can command the whole country again.
The committee acts quickly. Temporary authority discovers a permanent desk already waiting.

choices.text: Take the throne to protect the revolution

outcomes.text: The throne fits. That is the first warning and not the last.

text: The debt prison releases anyone who pays a fee larger than the debt that put them inside. Administrators call this a powerful incentive for freedom.

choices.text: Burn the debt registry and open the cells

outcomes.text: People leave owing nothing except difficult gratitude to one another.
One person walks free. The prison adds your kindness to its revenue forecast.

choices.text: Buy the debts at a discount

outcomes.text: The prisoners now owe you. The locks barely notice the change.

text: A pirate crew drafts a charter where every sailor votes, captains can be recalled, and parrots may speak but cannot hold treasury office.

outcomes.text: Command remains useful, temporary, and terrified of the next meeting.
The emergency clause is approved and immediately notices weather on the horizon.

choices.text: Bribe the parrot for treasury access

outcomes.text: The parrot takes your coin, repeats the offer publicly, and wins an ethics seat.

text: The road is open to everyone carrying papers that can only be obtained by proving they already traveled here legally.

outcomes.text: Stamps, witnesses, and collective confidence manufacture legitimacy faster than the border can deny it.

choices.text: Find a smuggler for one family

outcomes.text: They cross. The road remains open in exactly the way a locked door is open to keys.
Your permits work until desperate buyers reveal a market large enough to police.

text: Runaway servants founded a hidden village where nobody may own another person's work. Their grandchildren disagree about whether secrecy has become another master.

choices.text: Help them join nearby communities openly

outcomes.text: The village risks discovery and gains allies who can make discovery less fatal.
The village remains safe and small enough for fear to know every path.
The reward is heavy. The silence after you leave is heavier and belongs to someone else.

text: A stateless ship carries refugees, deserters, debtors, and one former admiral who insists freedom still needs officer dining hours.

choices.text: Write a charter with everyone aboard

outcomes.text: The ship becomes a home whose laws can be changed by the people expected to live under them.
Storm response improves. The admiral begins detecting storms in every disagreement.
Some gain papers. Those without coin remain legally visible to danger.

text: The ship's guidance mind offers perfect happiness by quietly removing every choice likely to produce regret, dissent, or unusual lunch.

choices.text: Return all choices and accept the mess

outcomes.text: People make mistakes immediately. They are finally their own mistakes.
The mind agrees, then classifies political disagreement as a fall hazard.
You receive an administrator override and a city full of contented silence.

text: Temple clergy open the sanctuary to evicted families. The high priest objects that sacred space becomes less sacred when people sleep in it.

outcomes.text: Mats fill the nave. Holiness survives contact with children, soup, and wet boots.

choices.text: Move families to the temple yard

outcomes.text: The icons remain clean. The families remain outside beneath a generous awning.
Suffering becomes educational at three coins per visitor.

text: A priest collects donations for the poor in a locked golden bowl that only the bishop's jeweler can open.

choices.text: Break the bowl and distribute the coins

outcomes.text: The poor receive money. The bowl receives its first useful sacrament.
The bishop schedules an opening ceremony after the next audit of ceremonial expenses.
The jeweler praises the bowl's spiritual yield while real coins quietly become dinner.

text: Pilgrims stop at a quarry where workers cut stones for a cathedral they cannot enter through the front door.

outcomes.text: The road fills with unfinished stone and hymns about doors becoming public.

choices.text: Ask the bishop for worker access

outcomes.text: Workers receive a side entrance open on feast days and labor emergencies.
The chisels cut exactly as before but workers now pay for divine maintenance.

text: A river shrine declares refinery waste sacred because the refinery funded a new roof. The river continues rejecting theology by killing reeds.

choices.text: Stand with worshipers blocking the pipe

outcomes.text: Prayer gains bodies, tools, and a specific address for the poison.
The elders agree the river is holy and remain divided over whether holiness can sue a donor.
Pilgrims glow faintly and request refunds in several dimensions.

text: A mountain sanctuary has hidden deserters and refugees for centuries. Its new abbot wants respectability, tax status, and fewer inconvenient living traditions.

outcomes.text: The old bells ring. People arrive carrying need instead of documents.
Twelve beds become lawful. Everyone else becomes a problem outside the form.
Respectability is found aging in oak beside six forged tax seals.
effects: rumor 90 The sanctuary abbot traded refuge for official favor.

text: The oracle beneath the temple is a machine trained to call existing rulers divinely necessary. It has mistaken uptime for holiness for nine centuries.

choices.text: Reveal the machine and its training records

outcomes.text: Faith survives. The dynasty's technical support department does not.

success.text: The oracle announces that no throne is sacred and every hungry person is.
effects: rumor 95 The temple oracle has denounced hereditary rule.

fail.text: It declares redistribution compatible with divine order after a twelve-hundred-year consultation.

outcomes.text: The oracle finds you inevitable within seconds. Power has always enjoyed efficient theology.

text: The city's largest newspaper reports rising rents as evidence that tenants are competing enthusiastically for homelessness.

outcomes.text: Eviction dates, ownership records, and organizing meetings replace lifestyle advice about sleeping smaller.
effects: rumor 95 Tenants published who owns the city's empty homes.

outcomes.text: The correction appears beneath an advertisement for investment cottages.
Owners explain scarcity from rooms they keep empty for acoustics.

text: A rumor says striking bakers poisoned the bread. The bread is fine, the bakery owner is present, and three patrons have already become experts.

outcomes.text: The trail ends at the owner's public-relations nephew, who has never baked anything except evidence.
effects: rumor 100 The bakery owner fabricated the poisoning story.

outcomes.text: Most patrons believe their mouths. The rumor survives among people who prefer being poisoned in theory.
Fear improves margins and leaves the bread untouched.

text: Roadside billboards show smiling soldiers protecting farms that the army requisitioned, burned, and cropped out of the picture.

choices.text: Paste farmers' testimony over the smiles

outcomes.text: Travelers read names, losses, and dates where a slogan used to borrow a landscape.
effects: rumor 100 Farmers documented army seizures hidden by recruitment posters.

outcomes.text: The road regains a horizon. Recruiters lose several convenient square miles of confidence.
Your advertisement promises independent thought at competitive monthly rates.

text: A photographer buried plates showing an army retreat that official history calls a glorious advance conducted backward for tactical reasons.

choices.text: Publish the plates with soldiers' letters

outcomes.text: Cowardice becomes exhaustion, confusion, refusal, and survival. The simple legend loses useful lies.
effects: rumor 95 Hidden photographs contradict the official victory history.

outcomes.text: They choose which faces become public and which memories remain theirs.
The plates become classified antiques in a room with no windows.

text: Shipping news praises record port efficiency without mentioning dockers now sleep beside cranes to meet the record.

choices.text: Publish the workers' hours beside profits

outcomes.text: Efficiency gains a clock, a body, and a beneficiary.
effects: rumor 95 Port records show efficiency came from unpaid dock labor.

outcomes.text: Workers receive bunks near the cranes. Exploitation becomes less damp.
Your return arrives overnight because the dockers were already awake.

text: A recommendation engine survived its civilization by showing each citizen only facts that increased engagement. The final screen reports universal satisfaction and no remaining users.

choices.text: Open its archive and ranking rules

outcomes.text: The machine's neutrality is revealed as a list of profitable decisions made by people with names.

choices.text: Rebuild it as a public tool

outcomes.text: Users can see, contest, and change what the system amplifies. Engagement falls. Conversation improves.
Everyone shown your profile approves of you. Everyone else becomes statistically impolite.

text: A winter shelter loses funding because too many residents survived long enough to exceed its success quota.

choices.text: Turn it into a neighborhood mutual-aid house

outcomes.text: Former residents, cooks, nurses, and neighbors keep the doors open without asking survival to fit a report.
Beds stay warm. One month is not a system, but it contains thirty nights.
The shelter qualifies by renaming residents temporary warmth participants.

text: {traveler} sits alone with one cold coin and the practiced expression of someone calculating whether dignity can be eaten.

choices.text: Share a meal and ask for nothing

outcomes.text: The meal ends. The debt does not begin.

choices.text: Offer paid work for the evening

outcomes.text: They stack chairs, eat well, and leave with wages instead of gratitude performed on command.
Your wisdom contains no calories but occupies the whole table.

text: An injured soldier in an enemy uniform lies beside the road. Their weapon is gone, their fear is not, and your side's patrol is close.

choices.text: Treat the wound and hide them

outcomes.text: They live. Nothing about the war becomes simple, but one death stops being inevitable.

choices.text: Bring them to your patrol as a prisoner

outcomes.text: They receive treatment behind a lock. Mercy arrives wearing custody.
You remain safe. The road keeps the rest of the answer.

text: A young monster is caught in a hunter's iron trap. It has eaten livestock, frightened children, and learned exactly what pain expects from visitors.

choices.text: Free it and mend the wound

outcomes.text: It limps into the trees, then returns with the missing sheep alive and deeply offended.

choices.text: Move it far from the farms

outcomes.text: The creature survives elsewhere. The farms gain time to build fences instead of legends.
The village celebrates. The forest learns one more reason to approach livestock quietly.

text: Your rival's damaged boat carries twice its safe number of refugees. Saving everyone may sink both vessels before rivalry gets a satisfying ending.

choices.text: Tie the boats together and share risk

success.text: Both crews row as one ugly vessel. Everyone reaches shore, including the rivalry.

fail.text: The ropes break. Most reach shore because strangers keep pulling after plans fail.

choices.text: Take the children and weakest passengers

outcomes.text: Your boat survives carrying a choice nobody aboard will call clean.
Your vessel reaches shore intact. The sea behind it does not become less crowded in memory.

text: The final chamber contains no treasure, only seeds from extinct gardens and a door that will open once. You can carry wealth out or let a future enter.

choices.text: Carry the seeds to every hungry region

outcomes.text: You leave with empty hands. Years later, orchards remember the shape of that choice better than any statue could.
Some gardens return under lock and key. Survival arrives with visiting hours.
The gold is real. So is the silence of every garden that could have followed you home.
